questions.question: Under which of the following Articles of the Constitution of India has the Supreme Court of India placed the Right to Privacy?
questions.solution:
The Supreme Court of India, in the landmark Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India (2017) case, unanimously declared the Right to Privacy as an intrinsic part of the Right to Life and Personal Liberty under Article 21.
  • Article 21: Guarantees that no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.
  • Article 15: Prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth.
  • Article 16: Provides equality of opportunity in matters of public employment.
  • Article 19: Guarantees the protection of certain rights regarding freedom of speech, assembly, etc.
Note: While privacy is protected under Article 21, it is not an absolute right and is subject to reasonable restrictions.

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is correct as per the Constitution of India?
questions.option_1: Inter-State trade and commerce is a State subject under the State List.
questions.option_2: Inter-State migration is a State subject under the State List.
questions.option_3: Inter-State quarantine is a Union subject under the Union List.
questions.option_4: Corporation tax is a State subject under the State List.
questions.solution:
The division of powers between the Union and States is detailed in the Seventh Schedule.
  • Inter-State quarantine: This is a Union subject (Entry 81 of the Union List), meaning only the Parliament has the power to legislate on it.
  • Inter-State trade and commerce: This is also a Union subject (Entry 42 of the Union List), making the statement regarding it being a State subject incorrect.
  • Inter-State migration: This falls under the Union List (Entry 81), not the State List.
  • Corporation tax: This is a Union subject (Entry 85 of the Union List); states generally collect taxes like Land Revenue or State GST.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements are correct about the Constitution of India?
  • 1. Powers of the Municipalities are given in Part IX A of the Constitution
  • 2. Emergency provisions are given in Part XVIII of the Constitution
  • 3. Provisions related to the amendment of the Constitution are given in Part XX of the Constitution
Select the answer using the code given below
questions.solution:
All three statements correctly describe the structural parts of the Constitution of India:
  • Part IX A: Added by the 74th Amendment Act (1992), it contains provisions related to Municipalities (Articles 243-P to 243-ZG).
  • Part XVIII: Contains Emergency Provisions (Articles 352-360), including National, State (President's Rule), and Financial emergencies.
  • Part XX: Contains Article 368, which dictates the power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and the procedure thereof.

questions.question:
The Constitution (71st Amendment) Act, 1992 amends the Eighth Schedule to the Constitution to include which of the following languages?
  • 1. Konkani
  • 2. Manipuri
  • 3. Nepali
  • 4. Maithili
Select the correct answer using the code given below
questions.solution:
The 71st Amendment Act, 1992, added three languages to the Eighth Schedule, often remembered by the mnemonic KMN:
  • Konkani
  • Manipuri
  • Nepali
The addition of these brought the total number of scheduled languages to 18 at that time. Maithili is incorrect in this context because it was added later by the 92nd Amendment Act, 2003 (along with Bodo, Dogri, and Santhali).

questions.question:
As per Article 368 of the Constitution of India, the Parliament may amend any provision of the Constitution by way of:
  • 1. Addition
  • 2. Variation
  • 3. Repeal
Select the correct answer using the code given below
questions.solution:
According to Article 368, the Parliament exercises its constituent power to amend the Constitution through three specific methods:
  • Addition: Adding new articles or clauses.
  • Variation: Modifying existing provisions.
  • Repeal: Removing existing provisions.
All three terms are explicitly mentioned in the text of Article 368(1).

questions.question: Who was the Provisional President of the Constituent Assembly before Dr. Rajendra Prasad took over?
questions.solution:
Following the French practice, the Constituent Assembly appointed the oldest member as the temporary or Provisional President.
  • Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha: He presided over the first meeting on December 9, 1946.
  • Dr. Rajendra Prasad: He was elected as the permanent President on December 11, 1946.
  • Dr. B.R. Ambedkar: He was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee, not the President of the Assembly.
  • C. Rajagopalachari: He was a prominent leader and later the first Indian Governor-General, but did not serve as Provisional President.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements in respect of the Constitution Day:
  • Statement-I: The Constitution Day is celebrated on 26th November every year to promote constitutional values among citizens
  • Statement-II: On 26th November, 1949, the Constituent Assembly of India set up a Drafting Committee under the Chairmanship of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar to prepare a Draft Constitution of India
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.option_1: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement-I
questions.option_2: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I
questions.option_3: Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
questions.option_4: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct
questions.solution:
Statement-I is correct: Constitution Day (Samvidhan Divas) is celebrated on 26th November to commemorate the adoption of the Constitution in 1949 and spread awareness of constitutional values.
Statement-II is incorrect: While a Drafting Committee was indeed set up under Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, it was established on August 29, 1947, not November 26, 1949. On November 26, 1949, the drafting process was actually completed and the Constitution was adopted.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
  • Statement-I: The Supreme Court of India has held in some judgements that the reservation policies made under Article 16(4) of the Constitution of India would be limited by Article 335 for maintenance of efficiency of administration
  • Statement-II: Article 335 of the Constitution of India defines the term 'efficiency of administration'
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.option_1: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement-I
questions.option_2: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I
questions.option_3: Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
questions.option_4: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct
questions.solution:
Statement-I is correct: The Supreme Court (e.g., in the M. Nagaraj case) has held that while Article 16(4) empowers the state to make reservations, Article 335 acts as a qualifying factor to ensure the efficiency of administration is not compromised.
Statement-II is incorrect: While Article 335 mentions that claims of SCs/STs shall be taken into consideration 'consistently with the maintenance of efficiency of administration,' the Constitution does not define what 'efficiency of administration' actually means. Definitions are often provided through judicial interpretation.

questions.question: In India, which one of the following Constitutional Amendments was widely believed to be enacted to overcome the judicial interpretations of the Fundamental Rights?
questions.solution:
The 42nd Amendment Act (1972), often called the 'Mini-Constitution,' was enacted during the Emergency primarily to assert Parliamentary supremacy over the judiciary.
  • It sought to limit the power of the courts to review laws and was a direct response to various judgements (like Kesavananda Bharati) that restricted Parliament's power to alter Fundamental Rights.
  • 1st Amendment: Aimed at land reforms and protecting them from judicial review via the Ninth Schedule.
  • 44th Amendment: Largely reversed the changes made by the 42nd Amendment to restore democratic checks.
  • 86th Amendment: Made Right to Education a Fundamental Right.

questions.question: Which one of the following statements best reflects the Chief purpose of the 'Constitution' of a country?
questions.option_1: It determines the objective for the making of necessary laws.
questions.option_2: It enables the creation of political offices and a government.
questions.option_3: It defines and limits the powers of government.
questions.option_4: It secures social justice, social equality and social security.
questions.solution:
While a Constitution serves many roles, its chief or fundamental purpose in a constitutional democracy is to define and limit the powers of government.
  • This concept is known as Limited Government or Constitutionalism. It prevents the state from becoming tyrannical by setting boundaries that the government cannot cross.
  • Other options like creating offices or securing social justice are functions of a Constitution, but the core essence of having a formal 'Constitution' is to ensure that the exercise of power is regulated and not arbitrary.

questions.question: In essence, what does 'Due Process of Law' mean?
questions.solution:
The concept of 'Due Process of Law' implies that a law must not only be enacted following the correct procedure but must also be fair, just, and reasonable.
  • Principle of Natural Justice: This is the core of due process, ensuring 'audi alteram partem' (hear the other side) and unbiased adjudication.
  • Procedure established by law: This is the literal interpretation of Article 21, but the Supreme Court in the Maneka Gandhi case (1978) ruled that such procedure must also be 'due' or 'fair'.
  • Fair application of law: While related, 'due process' specifically checks the substance of the law itself, not just its application.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
  • According to the Constitution of India, the Central Government has a duty to protect States from internal disturbances.
  • The Constitution of India exempts the States from providing legal counsel to a person being held for preventive detention.
  • According to the Prevention of Terrorism Act, 2002, confession of the accused before the police cannot be used as evidence.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.solution:
This question tests knowledge of Constitutional duties and specific statutes:
  • Statement 1: Correct. Article 355 states it is the duty of the Union to protect every State against external aggression and internal disturbance.
  • Statement 2: Correct. Article 22 (Preventive Detention) specifies that the right to consult a legal practitioner does not apply to a person detained under preventive detention laws.
  • Statement 3: Incorrect. Under the POTA (2002) (now repealed), confessions made to a police officer (not below the rank of SP) were admissible as evidence, contrary to the general rule in the Evidence Act.
Since two statements are correct, the answer is 'Only two'.

questions.question: 'Right to Privacy' is protected under which Article of the Constitution of India?
questions.solution:
The 'Right to Privacy' is an integral part of the right to life and personal liberty.
  • Article 21: The Supreme Court in Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) held that privacy is a fundamental right protected under this Article.
  • Article 15: Deals with non-discrimination.
  • Article 19: Protects six freedoms (speech, assembly, etc.).
  • Article 29: Protects the interests of minorities.

questions.question: A legislation that confers on the executive or administrative authority an unguided and uncontrolled discretionary power in the matter of the application of law violates which one of the following Articles of the Constitution of India?
questions.solution:
Granting 'unguided and uncontrolled' power to an authority violates the principle of Equality before Law.
  • Article 14: Prohibits arbitrariness. If a law allows an official to act without clear guidelines, it leads to discrimination and unequal treatment.
  • Article 32: Relates to Constitutional Remedies (Writs).
  • Article 44: Relates to the Uniform Civil Code (DPSP).

questions.question: Which one of the following in Indian polity is an essential feature that indicates that it is federal in character?
questions.option_1: The independence of judiciary is safeguarded.
questions.option_2: The Union Legislature has elected representatives from constituent units.
questions.option_3: The Union Cabinet can have elected representatives from regional parties
questions.option_4: The Fundamental Rights enforceable by Courts of Law.
questions.solution:
A Federal System is characterized by a division of powers between the Center and Units.
  • Independence of Judiciary: This is essential to resolve disputes between the Center and States and to protect the Supremacy of the Constitution.
  • Other options: While regional parties and elected representatives are features of Indian democracy, they do not strictly define the federal structure as effectively as an independent judiciary does.

questions.question: What is the position of the Right to Property in India?
questions.option_1: Legal right available to citizens only
questions.option_2: Legal right available to any person
questions.option_3: Fundamental Right available to citizens only
questions.option_4: Neither Fundamental Right nor legal right
questions.solution:
Initially a Fundamental Right under Article 31, the Right to Property was transformed by the 44th Amendment Act (1978).
  • It is now a Legal Right (also called a Constitutional Right) under Article 300-A.
  • It is available to any person (including non-citizens and legal entities like corporations), not just citizens.

questions.question: What was the exact constitutional status of India on 26th January, 1950?
questions.solution:
On January 26, 1950, the Preamble described India as a 'Sovereign Democratic Republic'.
  • The words 'Socialist' and 'Secular' were added later by the 42nd Amendment Act (1976).
  • Therefore, the 'exact' status at the time of commencement did not include those two terms.

questions.question: Which one of the following best defines the term 'State'?
questions.option_1: A community of persons permanently occupying a definite territory independent of external control and possessing an organized government
questions.option_2: A politically organized people of a definite territory and possessing an authority to govern them, maintain law and order, protect their natural rights and safeguard their means of sustenance
questions.option_3: A number of persons who have been living in a definite territory for a very long time with their own culture, tradition and government
questions.option_4: A society permanently living in a definite territory with a central authority, an executive responsible to the central authority and an independent judiciary
questions.solution:
In political science, a 'State' must possess four essential elements: Population, Territory, Government, and Sovereignty.
  • Option 1: This is the most comprehensive definition as it mentions 'independent of external control' (Sovereignty) and 'organized government'.
  • Option 2: Focuses more on the functions of a state rather than its definition.
  • Note: Sovereignty is the soul of the state that distinguishes it from other associations.

questions.question: Which one of the following factors constitutes the best safeguard of liberty in a liberal democracy?
questions.solution:
The 'Separation of Powers' (Executive, Legislature, and Judiciary) is the best safeguard of liberty.
  • It ensures a system of checks and balances, preventing any single branch from becoming despotic.
  • Committed Judiciary: Often implies a judiciary committed to the government's ideology, which actually threatens liberty.
  • Centralization: Leads to the concentration of power, which is the opposite of liberty.

questions.question: Under the Indian Constitution, the concentration of wealth violates
questions.option_2: the Directive Principles of State Policy
questions.solution:
The prevention of concentration of wealth is a specific directive under the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP).
  • Article 39(c): Explicitly states that the operation of the economic system should not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment.

questions.question: Constitutional government means
questions.option_1: a representative government of a nation with the federal structure
questions.option_2: a government whose Head enjoys nominal powers
questions.option_3: a government whose Head enjoys real powers
questions.option_4: a government limited by the terms of the Constitution
questions.solution:
A Constitutional Government is essentially a Limited Government.
  • It means the government derives its power from the Constitution and is bound by the limitations set by it. It cannot act beyond the authority granted to it.

questions.question: A constitutional government by definition is a
questions.solution: By definition, Constitutionalism (Constitutional Government) denotes Limited Government. This means the authority of the state is restricted by a body of fundamental law (the Constitution) to protect individual rights.

questions.question: Which one of the following categories of Fundamental Rights incorporates protection against untouchability as a form of discrimination?
questions.solution:
The Right to Equality (Articles 14–18) includes Article 17, which deals with the 'Abolition of Untouchability'.
  • It is the only Fundamental Right that is absolute in nature and forbids the practice of untouchability in any form.

questions.question: In India, the separation of the judiciary from the executive is enjoined by
questions.option_2: a directive principle of state policy
questions.solution: Article 50 of the Indian Constitution, which is a Directive Principle of State Policy (DPSP), directs the State to take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State.

questions.question:
Other than the Fundamental Rights, which of the following parts of the Constitution of India reflect/reflect the principles and provisions of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948)?
  1. Preamble
  2. Directive principles of state policy
  3. Fundamental duties
Select the correct answer using the given code below:
questions.solution:
The UDHR (1948) influenced the drafting of the Indian Constitution significantly:
  • Preamble: Reflects the spirit of dignity and liberty.
  • DPSP: Reflects economic, social, and cultural rights (e.g., right to work, education).
  • Fundamental Duties: Reflects the UDHR's principle that everyone has duties to the community.
Thus, all three parts reflect UDHR principles.
